title: NVR, Inc. (NVR) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of NVR, Inc. (NVR) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 5.0% free cash flow growth rate is assumed. This is driven by the structural undersupply of housing in the US, providing a long-term tailwind for new construction. NVR's asset-light model ensures that top-line growth translates efficiently into massive cash generation, currently exceeding $1.1B.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: An 8.0% discount rate is applied. While housing is a cyclical industry, NVR's avoidance of land development risk significantly lowers its operational leverage. The company's pristine balance sheet and predictable cash flows justify a lower discount rate than traditional, asset-heavy homebuilders.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.0% terminal growth rate reflects long-term economic and population growth. The demand for housing is fundamentally tied to household formation, making a rate matching long-term GDP growth appropriate.

thesisParagraphs: NVR is often misunderstood simply as a homebuilder. In reality, it is a highly efficient capital compounding machine that happens to build houses. The brilliance of NVR's model lies in its use of lot options. Instead of buying raw land, navigating years of zoning, and taking on massive development risk, NVR pays a non-refundable deposit to secure finished lots from developers just-in-time.

This strategy structurally transforms the economics of homebuilding. NVR requires minimal invested capital, resulting in industry-leading Returns on Invested Capital (ROIC). Because the business needs so little cash to operate and grow, management aggressively repurchases shares. This relentless reduction in share count, combined with a structural housing shortage driving demand, makes NVR highly attractive and undervalued even at its optically high share price.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why is NVR's stock price so high ($6000+)?
faq.answer: NVR has never split its stock, and it aggressively repurchases shares, constantly reducing the supply. The high absolute share price is irrelevant to its valuation; the company's market capitalization and earnings power are what matter, and its EPS is extremely high due to the low share count.

faq.question: What is the "lot option" strategy?
faq.answer: Unlike traditional builders who buy land years in advance, NVR pays a fee (usually 10% of the lot price) to a developer for the right, but not the obligation, to buy a finished lot in the future. This limits NVR's downside risk if the housing market crashes, as they can simply walk away from the option.

faq.question: Is NVR still a good buy during high interest rates?
faq.answer: High rates hurt affordability, but they also create a "lock-in effect" where existing homeowners won't sell to lose their low-rate mortgages. This severely limits existing home inventory, driving buyers toward new construction like NVR's, supporting the undervalued verdict.
